issues.comment: BlockedEmail.should_block? method has a race condition where multiple concurrent calls with the same email can read the same match_count value, both increment it, and both save, causing incorrect statistics (e.g. 2 calls incrementing from 5 results in match_count=6 instead of 7).
record.save call ignores return value and potential failures, causing statistics updates to fail silently if save encounters validation errors or database issues.

result.values.email access is not null-checked before pushObject call - if result.values.email is undefined/null, it will push undefined into rejectedEmails array, causing subsequent contains() checks to potentially misbehave.

JavaScript uses I18n.t('user.email.invalid') for blocked emails but server-side EmailValidator uses I18n.t(:'user.email.blocked'), causing users to see different error messages for the same blocked email condition depending on client vs server-side validation.

Email matching in BlockedEmail.where(email: email) is likely case-sensitive, but email addresses should typically be treated case-insensitively - users could bypass blocks by changing email case (e.g. User@SPAM.com vs user@spam.com).
rejectedEmails.contains(email) check is case-sensitive, allowing users to bypass client-side blocked email detection by changing email case between attempts.

EmailValidator test only stubs BlockedEmail.should_block? but doesn't test the whitelist/blacklist validation logic that runs first in the validator, providing incomplete test coverage of the validator's full behavior.
